entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 6)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

💬What would you ask someone who's led SAP Finance transformations and global rollouts?

After years working on SAP Finance, international SAP rollouts, and project management, I've realized something: the most valuable insights don't always come from presentations or documentation—they come from honest conversations.

Every implementation brings new challenges. Every SAP rollout teaches unexpected lessons. And every question, no matter how simple or complex, has the potential to help someone else facing the same situation.

That's why I'm excited to participate in Session 6 of my Ask Me Anything (AMA) series! 🎉

This is your opportunity to ask me anything about:
✅SAP Finance (FI/CO)
✅Global SAP rollouts
✅Project & Program Management
✅SAP transformation and organizational change
✅Managing international stakeholders and cross-functional teams
✅Lessons learned from real-life SAP implementations
✅Building a successful career in SAP consulting and leadership

Whether you're preparing for your first rollout, leading a transformation program, tackling a challenging project, or simply curious about life in the SAP world, I'd love to hear your questions.

📌Leave your question in the comments.

🔒Prefer to stay anonymous? You can submit your question here: Ask Me Anything – Daniel Mateo

Do not miss the opportunity and submit it before 10th of August 2026! Throughout the month, I'll select questions to answer in a dedicated video recap, sharing practical advice, real project experiences, and lessons learned from the field. 

Let's learn from each other and keep strengthening the SAP community—one question at a time.

Looking forward to reading your questions! 🚀

entry.title: Re: Error when trying to add new member to account dimens...
entry.content:
Nasser, You should be able to see the note. But I am pasting the content from it.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Symptom

  • Below error message would appear when saving or creating the seamless planning public account dimension in SAC system:
    Failed to execute select "VALUE" as "HIERARCHY"
    from "TENANT_XXXX"."sap.fpa.services.modeling::SplitToTable"('parentId')
    where "VALUE" not in (
    select "HIERARCHY" from "XXXXXX"
    where "NODEID" = ?
    ). Component = DbHelper. Reason = invalid schema name: XXXXX: line 2 col 23 (at pos 52) 
  • This issue seems to only occur on the seamless planning Account dimension. This issue would not occur on seamless planning Generic dimension.
  • This issue would not occur on Local Public Dimensions as well

Environment

  • SAP Analytics Cloud  2026.8.16 
  • Datasphere System 2026.15.1 

Reproducing the Issue

  1. Log onto SAC
  2. Navigate to Modeler-> Public Dimension Tables
  3. Click on "Dimension Tables" to create a new public dimension table
  4. On the following "Data Storage Location" panel, select the Data Storage Location as SAP Datasphere space 
  5. On the following "Create Dimension Table" panel, select the type as Account and enter a name for this Account dimension
  6. Add a member into this dimension table and save this change
    => Observe below error message would appear:
    Failed to execute select "VALUE" as "HIERARCHY"
    from "XXXX"."sap.fpa.services.modeling::SplitToTable"('parentId')
    where "VALUE" not in (
    select "HIERARCHY" from "XXXXX"
    where "NODEID" = ?
    ). Component = DbHelper. Reason = invalid schema name: XXXXX: line 2 col 23 (at pos 52) 

Cause

This issue is under investigation by development.

Resolution

This issue is under investigation by development.

entry.title: Re: PO Workflow Approval Clarification
entry.content:
Hello @ashish_malik2 

Assuming you are using the classic workflow scenario Releasing External Purchasing Docs. (MM-PUR-GF), at any given moment you have only one instance of workflow active, for the current release code. It doesn't matter how many release codes you have in a given release strategy, only one instance of the workflow is active. Release levels / codes are defined in PO release strategy configuration, not in the workflow definition - that's important.

Here is the workflow diagram:

 

If a release strategy has multiple release codes / levels, then the workflow is started for the 1st release code/level. When the PO is approved on this level, the workflow terminates and a new instance of the workflow is started for the next level, etc. That is the right-hand side branch of the diagram.

If a purchase order is substantially changed, meaning that a new release strategy is determined for the PO (that is your case), then the active instance of the workflow is terminated (left-hand branch of the diagram) and a new workflow instance is started. Therefore you should not have any hanging instances on 3rd level as in your case. That is at least how the standard workflow scenario works.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Calculate hashbytes for table
entry.content:
I doubt the original poster will ever read this but, that's a very clever solution.   

However, one restriction for this approach is that, for the update @hash  statement, even though it's not actually updating any table, it still allocates rows in the transaction log (all within the single transaction that is an implicit part of the update statement).   

If you try and run this against a really large table, you'll fill up your transaction logs

entry.title: Special Tax conditions from components should roll-up to parent item for a BOM product in Sales
entry.content:
In sales order for our agronomy process, we are using a BOM with ERLA item category, which is then derived to TAQ in the sales order. The standard behavior of this product is to have the pricing at the parent item level, and the components are mainly used to know the products of the blend we will be creating.

However, in this scenario, each component will have their own agronomy taxes, which must be rolled up to the parent item for legal compliance, and these taxes cannot be manually added either. We cannot just set a condition record for the parent item on these taxes, since the components will be different for every sales order.

Is there a way to pull these taxes to the parent item?

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Create Sales Order with ERLA product
  2. Add pricing to parent item
  3. Confirm taxes are added at component level
  4. Save Sales Order
  5. Create Outbound Delivery
  6. PGI OBD
  7. Create Billing document

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
1.

const cds = require('@sap/cds')

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
      
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @TO: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @TO: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @TO: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @TO: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
    this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      if (player.position != 100) return req.error(409, 'Player must be in square 100 to win the game')

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        .set({
          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          winner_ID: player.ID
        })
        .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    })

    return super.init()
  }
}

2.

3.

4.
I have added logic which returns http 409 with information that user must be in square 100 in order to win the game.

entry.title: User with Display-Only PO Catalog Able to Create PO
entry.content:
We have identified an authorization issue in our Production system where user was able to create Purchase Order despite being assigned only the display business catalog SAP_MM_BC_PURCH_DOC_DSP_PC through business role BR_WAREHOUSE_CLERK.

Our analysis shows that the application is available only via the display catalog. However, the user also receives BSART (Purchase Document Type) authorization through other assigned business roles  which appears to have enabled Purchase Order creation.

From an authorization perspective, a display-only application should not allow Create actions simply because another business role grants the underlying authorization object. Combining a display-only application with write authorizations from unrelated business roles results in unintended business access.

Please investigate this behavior and confirm whether this is a standard SAP design or an authorization defect. If this is expected behavior, please advise the SAP-recommended standard approach to ensure that users assigned the display-only Purchase Order catalog cannot create Purchase Orders in such scenarios.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: User with Display-Only PO Catalog Able to Create PO
entry.content:
Hi,

Refer to the KBA 3376349 - How to split Create/Change authorization for Purchase Orders - SAP for Me

The behavior is consistent with standard SAP design.

The attached KBA explains that Create (Activity 01) and Change (Activity 02) are both mapped to the same write access category and cannot be separated using standard authorization configuration. Since the user receives write authorization through other assigned business roles (for example, via BSART authorization), the backend authorization permits Purchase Order creation even when the application is accessed from a display business catalog.

If the business requirement is to prevent creation while allowing display access, this cannot be achieved through standard authorizations alone. As described in the KBA, a customer-specific implementation of BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PO_OPEN can be used to enforce display mode based on custom logic.

Note: You could implement the BAdI BD_MMPUR_FINAL_CHECK_PO to introduce custom validation logic during Purchase Order save. This allows enforcing business rules and preventing unauthorized modifications even if technical access exists.

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Inquiry for sales document type in SAP S4 HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Dear @guoqing_yang861,

Public Cloud now allows customers using SAP Central Business Configuration to create custom sales order types through Configure Standard Order Type. However, these document types are created from the predefined XTA template, which is based on the standard order type TA/OR. The available configuration remains limited, so this functionality does not provide a general “copy as” capability for all standard sales document types

More details below:

SAP Help – Custom Sales Order Types

SAP Help – Create a Sales Document Type

SAP KBA 3717002 – Configure Standard Order Type Feature in SAP Central Business Configuration

For actual after sales service execution, SAP recommends using Service Management transactions, rather than creating additional SD sales document types:

  • SVO1 – Service Order supports time-and-material items as well as fixed-price service items
  • SVO2 – Fixed Price Service Order is intended for service orders processed on a fixed-price basis

These are service transaction types processed in Service Management and are different from SD sales document types such as OR, CBFD and CBAR

More details below:

SAP Help – Service Orders

SAP Help – Item Categories for Service Orders and In-House Repairs

SAP does not provide a separate standard transaction type called a free-of-charge service order. Where service work is performed without charging the customer, this should normally be represented through the pricing or coverage of the SVO1/SVO2 service process. CBFD – Delivery Free of Charge remains appropriate when the requirement is to deliver physical products or replacement materials free of charge, but it does not manage service execution, technician confirmations, service parts or service costs

More details below:

SAP Help – Sales Orders Without Charge

For a customer product that is returned for inspection or repair the standard process is:

  1. Create a CBAR – Standard Return
  2. Select the logistical follow-up activity 0026 – In-House Repair (Service)
  3. The system creates an In-House Repair document from which the repair is processed using RPO1 – Repair Order or RPO2 – Fixed Price Repair Order

CBAR manages the physical customer-return logistics, while RPO1/RPO2 manage the actual repair activities. They are complementary documents, not alternative sales order types

More details below:

SAP Help – Create In-House Repairs in Customer Returns

SAP Help – Manage the Returns Process for Repair Objects

SAP Help – Manage In-House Repairs

SAP Learning Explaining the Integration Between an In-House Repair and Logistics

A custom return order type based on CBAR is not supported. Advanced Returns Management depends on predefined copying control and follow-on document integration, which customers cannot configure for a custom document type in Public Edition

More details below:

SAP KBA 3759318 – Custom Return Order Type Creation Based on CBAR Not Supported

Therefore the recommended standard mapping is:

  • After sales service performed at the customer site: SVO1 or SVO2
  • Free of charge service work: SVO1 or SVO2 with the appropriate pricing or service coverage
  • Free of charge delivery of a physical product: CBFD
  • Customer return followed by workshop repair: CBAR followed by RPO1 or RPO2

Summary:

There are no other standard SD sales document types that directly replace OR, CBFD and CBAR for these requirements. The correct approach is to use Service Management transaction types SVO1/SVO2 and RPO1/RPO2 together with the standard SD return or free-of-charge processes where required

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Metadata error in BTP Cockpit when trying to reach S3...
entry.content:
Hello. 

Here are typically the top 5 common issues/resolutions:

1) Destination Configuration -> The BTP Destination is misconfigured, missing or has incorrect authentication settings.
|__WebIDEEnabled = true
|__HTML5.DynamicDestination = true

2) The Backend Odata -> service is not activated in the backend -> Tcode /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and ensure that the ICF node is active in Tcode SICF

3) Authorization is missing -> The user or tech user used by the destination lacks the necessary authorization object to access the service or the underlying metadata.  Check Tcode SU53.

4) Cloud connector -> If connecting in on-premise system, the SAP Cloud Connector tunnel or channel might be down or misconfigured -> Check SAP Cloud Connector SCC Logs

5) Cache may need to be check -> Sometimes Odata metadata cache in the Gateway needs to be cleaned up -> /IWFND/CACHE_CLEANUP or /IWBEP/CACHE_CLEANUP.

More information may be needed outside the above like HAR file traces and /IWFND/ERROR_LOGS or your Test connectivity test via BAS command curl -v -i "destinationName.dest/sap/opu/odata/..." to see the service directly bypassing any of your UI code.

entry.title: Re: failing since yesterday npm install @sap-rfc/node...
entry.content:
Debjani and team,

Your attachment shows red X marks in the Repository-Based Shipment Channel that your S user account or technical user does not have the necessary active licenses or permission for that specific repository.

You may need to logon to your Repository-Base Shipment channel and verify your licenses.

2) You may try to verify your .npmrc Configuration:
|__> npm error is showing --> @SAP-rfc/nod-rfc-library at [https://registry.npmjs.org/][https://registry.npmjs.org/)

You may need to make sure your project .npmrc file is correctly pointing or routing to @SAP-rfc

So in theory -> Your .npmrc should look similar to:
|__@sap-rfc:registry=[https://npm.sap.com/](https://npm.sap.com/) or the specific repository endpoint provided in your SAP Shipment channel dashboard.

You may double check your credentials.
|__> If your credential even were active yesterday, have expired or need refreshing, logon to your Repository Base Shipment Channel -> go to User Management -> regenerate or copy  your correct NPM Base 64 Credential for your tech user -> Update your .npmrc file

That is the theory so far.  If not, other team in the community may also assist.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Announcing General Availability of SAP Business Data Cloud Connect for Google BigQuery
entry.content:
Today, SAP is announcing the general availability (GA) of SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) Connect for Google BigQuery, delivering on our vision of a unified, AI-ready data ecosystem with Google Cloud. With bidirectional, zero-copy data sharing between Google BigQuery and SAP BDC, organizations can securely access trusted SAP business data directly from BigQuery while enriching SAP BDC with Google datasets—all without creating duplicate copies. The result is a unified, governed data foundation that preserves business context, reduces integration complexity, and accelerates analytics, intelligent applications, and AI across the enterprise.

SAP BDC Connect for Google BigQuery is now available for SAP Business Data Cloud deployments on AWS and Google Cloud Platform. Support for Azure is in our roadmap (Refer SAP Roadmap Explorer  for the latest information).

 

Unlocking Context-rich Data with Zero‑Copy integration:

SAP BDC is designed to help solve the challenge of fragmented and de‑contextualized data by curating SAP data into business‑ready data products that are managed by SAP and enriched with business semantics, lineage, and governance. SAP BDC Connect for Google BigQuery establishes a seamless, bidirectional, zero-copy connection between SAP BDC and Google BigQuery. Users can access shared SAP data products—complete with tables, metadata, and rich business semantics—directly inside the BigQuery UI and the Knowledge Catalog. They can also publish Google data products to SAP BDC, enabling trusted data products to be reused across analytics, intelligent applications and AI use cases.

Through this integration, organizations can:

  • Access trusted, context-rich SAP data products directly within Google BigQuery.
  • Share Google data products with SAP BDC.
  • Simplify their landscape by reducing costly data movement and duplication.

This approach significantly reduces data engineering efforts and total cost of ownership while ensuring data remains governed, consistent and up to date. 

High Level Architecture:

Configuring SAP BDC Connect for Google BigQuery:

Getting started with SAP BDC Connect for Google BigQuery begins with a one‑time enrollment process that establishes a secure, trusted connection between SAP BDC and your Google BigQuery environment.

The enrollment process initiates a secure, two-way handshake using mutual TLS (mTLS) and OpenID Connect (OIDC) to establish a trusted, authenticated connection between SAP BDC and Google BigQuery. The Google BigQuery service account serves as the trusted identity for the Google Cloud project, enabling secure authentication and authorization throughout the enrollment process.

The set up typically requires collaboration between a BigQuery administrator and an SAP administrator to complete. Listed below are the key steps.

  1. BigQuery Admin - Set up Enterprise Plus edition: Enable BigQuery Enterprise Plus edition and create a reservation when querying your SAP BDC instance.
  2. BigQuery Admin - Create the Lakehouse catalog: A BigQuery Administrator creates an empty Delta Sharing catalog to connect to SAP BDC. This catalog will represent the connection to SAP BDC. After creation, describe the catalog to retrieve the Google Cloud Service Account ID.
  3. SAP Admin - Configure the connector in SAP for Me: The SAP administrator provisions a SAP BDC Connect application instance using the Service Account ID and generates an invitation link. The invitation link contains both the SAP Connector Endpoint URL and the SAP Invitation Code. Provide the Connector Endpoint and the Invitation Code to BigQuery administrator to complete the catalog enrollment.
  4. BigQuery Admin - Update Catalog with SAP Enrollment Details: Complete the handshake by adding the endpoint and invitation code to the Lakehouse catalog.
  5. SAP Admin – Add the newly created SAP BDC Connect to appropriate system formation: SAP administrator adds the SAP BDC connect instance to the appropriate system formation in SAP for me to enable access to SAP data products from relevant SAP applications. This completes the set up and at this point, the secure connection is in place.
  6. SAP Users – Share SAP BDC data products with Google BigQuery: Your Data Stewards can begin sharing SAP data products from SAP BDC and consuming them in Google BigQuery. Access to the SAP BDC data through BigQuery is controlled by standard Google Cloud IAM permissions on the Catalog.

For more detailed information, please refer to the SAP Help Portal documentation and Google Help documentation.

 

Sharing SAP BDC Data Products with Google BigQuery:

Practitioners working in SAP BDC can securely share SAP data products with Google BigQuery through a simple, governed workflow.

To share a SAP data product with Google BigQuery, follow the steps below:

  1. Select a data product from the catalog in the SAP BDC Cockpit.
  2. Initiate the sharing process by clicking the ‘Share’ button.
  3. Choose the appropriate SAP BDC Connect Instance representing your Google BigQuery system and share.

When the data product is shared from SAP BDC to Google BigQuery, both the data and its associated metadata/semantic information is shared as well. The metadata describes the data product, including the data it contains, its business meaning, and their relationship to other data. This metadata is exposed in Google's Knowledge catalog. You can either use "SAP CSN Metadata" aspect type or select Catalog type then SAP subtype in the Knowledge Catalog search filter to discover SAP data products, making it easy for Google users to search, filter, discover, and use SAP data products across customer’s Google Cloud environment.

Demo Video: Sharing data products from SAP BDC to Google BigQuery.

 

 

 

Sharing Google BigQuery Data Products to SAP BDC:

The integration between SAP BDC and Google BigQuery is bidirectional, enabling a closed‑loop data lifecycle. With SAP BDC Connect, you can publish 2 types of assets from Google Cloud,  the curated data products from Knowledge Catalog or Apache Iceberg REST catalogs containing the iceberg tables . Once data products are published to SAP BDC, they become discoverable and reusable by downstream consumers across analytics, planning, and AI use cases, extending the value of insights generated in Google BigQuery throughout the SAP ecosystem. 

Currently, publishing from Google BigQuery to SAP BDC is done through the Google Command Line Interface (CLI). A new user interface for sharing to SAP BDC is in our roadmap.

You can find below the key steps for publishing data products from Google BigQuery to SAP BDC.

  1. Google User - Create Cloud Storage bucket: Create a Cloud Storage bucket to store your Iceberg metadata and data files. All resources, including the backend bucket, must exist in the same geographic region.
  2. Google User - Create Apache Iceberg REST catalog: Set up an Apache Iceberg REST catalog endpoint in the Lakehouse runtime catalog with credential vending enabled, containing the namespaces and tables you want to share.
  3. Google User - Create Data Product in Knowledge Catalog: A data product is a curated, logical grouping of data assets packaged together to solve specific business problems. Instead of publishing a full catalog, you can select specific Apache Iceberg tables, group them into a data product for publishing that curated collection to SAP BDC.
  4. Google Admin - Create Workload Identity Federation (WIF): To allow SAP BDC access to your Lakehouse resources, configure Workload Identity Federation (WIF). This establishes trust between Google Cloud and the SAP BDC OIDC issuer.
  5. Google User - Publish data product to SAP Business Data Cloud: You can publish the Apache Iceberg REST catalog directly or alternatively, you can publish a Knowledge Catalog data product. 
  6. SAP User - Access and install the shared data product in SAP BDC: SAP BDC users can discover the shared data products in the SAP BDC Catalog and install them in SAP Datasphere for further consumption in analytics/planning, intelligent applications or AI use cases within SAP Ecosystem.

For more detailed information, please refer to the SAP Help Portal documentation and Google Help documentation.

Demo Video:  Sharing Data Products from Google BigQuery to SAP BDC.

 

 

 

Summary:

In 2025, we delivered SAP BDC Connect for Databricks followed by SAP BDC Connect for Snowflake in early 2026. With the general availability of SAP BDC Connect for Google BigQuery, we continue to deliver on our vision of an open data ecosystem—one that enables customers to securely and seamlessly use their data across platforms. 

This is another step toward our commitment to giving customers the flexibility to choose technologies that best meet their business needs while preserving business context, governance, and trust. As we continue to expand the SAP BDC open data ecosystem, our focus remains on making access to trusted, contextual and governed data simple, secure, and scalable—empowering organizations to accelerate innovation without the loss of business context, data fragmentation and silos.

If you liked reading this post, please feel free to share it with your colleagues. Also, I invite you to follow the SAP Business Data Cloud tag to get the latest product news.

entry.title: Re: Data Management in the Age of AI: Lessons from the Field

entry.title: From pipelines to products- when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.
entry.content:
What SAP actually changed: SAP becomes the producer

Data products are not new; the data-mesh community has argued for them since around 2019. What SAP did in Business Data Cloud (BDC) is structural: for SAP sources, SAP itself becomes the producer.

SAP-managed data products ship with the platform. They are pre-built extractions from S/4HANA and other SAP applications, landed and refreshed by SAP-managed pipelines into the BDC storage layer (HDLF), and published to the catalog with their business semantics attached. You do not build the extraction or its base semantics. You activate them, and build only the logic that is genuinely yours on top, which moves your scarce engineering effort from plumbing to differentiation. Where data-team bandwidth is the binding constraint, and for most organizations it is, that is the whole game.

This is not "only SAP data." There are three doors into the same governed catalog. Only the first is gated by an application purchase.

 

Why “zero-copy” is a fact, not a slogan

The foundation stores data products as open columnar files (Parquet, managed as Delta tables) in a managed object store built on SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF). File-based is the key phrase. A warehouse locks data inside a proprietary format, so the only way to share it is to export a copy. An object store keeps data as open files in a bucket, and open files can be read by many engines at once without anyone copying anything.

This is the lakehouse principle applied inside SAP: one physical copy in open Delta format, read by SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks, and SAP HANA Cloud through a shared catalog. Inside the BDC boundary, that means produce once and consume across all three engines without duplicating the data. One copy, many readers, that is the mechanism the market calls "zero-copy."

The full pipeline: how data actually lands in HDLF

For technical audiences, here is the honest end-to-end. CDS views are the extraction interface throughout, but the pipeline runs in two contexts. In the SAP-managed context, SAP owns standard, SAP-released CDS views built on the One Domain Model, its cross-application semantic model that keeps core entities consistent. In the customer-managed context, you create or identify the CDS entity yourself: a standard view, an extended or Z-CDS view, a custom business object, or a table. The same physical machinery moves both, and that machinery is what the rest of this section walks through.

 

 

 

Extraction at a glance, by source system

 

Cloud S/4HANA activates SAP-managed data products directly; everything else, on-prem S/4HANA, ECC, and non-SAP, enters through the customer-managed door. The source differs, the entry point does not: CDS views over the Cloud Connector for on-prem, extractors and tables via a Datasphere connection for ECC, database and file connections for third-party. Whatever the origin, one Replication Flow performs the universal physical write into HDLF as Delta Parquet, landing in a single governed catalog.

 

One product, end to end, and back

Follow a single product from source to intelligence and back. It leaves S/4HANA through SAP-managed CDS extraction and lands in the BDC foundation as governed Delta files. It appears in the catalog carrying its schema, semantics, and lineage: the "app store" moment where you browse before you install. The customer activates it, making it consumable.

From there the same single product serves everything. Datasphere models it; SAP Analytics Cloud plans and reports on it; and when a data-science team wants it, it is shared to SAP Databricks over Delta Sharing, appearing in Unity Catalog as a mounted table while the bytes stay in the foundation. What crosses the share is the table and its column-level meaning: names, types, keys, and comments, so the data scientist sees labeled, described columns rather than naked ones. What does not cross is the multidimensional model or the source-side access controls, measures, hierarchies, and data-access rules live in Datasphere and SAC, not in the shared file, and are re-established on the consumer side. Zero-copy is real at the boundary; meaning travels at the column level, not the model level.

When the loop closes, the data product is published back into BDC as a new governed data product through a federated remote share, live and zero-copy, not a physical import.

One honest boundary

Good advisory work separates what a platform will show from what it will guarantee. So, plainly: Delta Sharing transports tables, not multidimensional models. Measures, hierarchies, and data-access controls do not survive the protocol and must be re-established on the consuming side. The semantics that carry over are the structural ones (keys, column names, comments, data types), not the full analytic model. Say this up front and you will be trusted; let people discover it later and you will not.

Go Deeper On Delta Sharing: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-managed-data-products-in-sap-bdc-what-really-happens-when-your/ba-p/14394675

The economics: what actually burns capacity

Most confusion about BDC cost comes from collapsing three things that are actually separate. Hold them apart and the economics stop being mysterious. This is the distinction solution architects and commercial teams have to be able to explain to a customer.

Illustrative split from a small worked example. Exact mix depends on data volume, refresh frequency, and workloads run.

The single most misunderstood point in the whole model: intelligent content does not consume capacity units. You license the application, but what burns capacity is the data products and infrastructure underneath it. Switching on more applications barely moves the meter. Cost follows the workloads you actually run on your data (provisioning, refresh, queries, and any optional ML in Databricks), not the number of applications you own.

Why this matters now

The reason the industry is talking about governed data products in 2026 is not nostalgia for data-mesh theory. It is AI. Agents and copilots fail in production mostly because they lack business context: they do not know that this table means settlement, or that this field is a posted invoice. A governed data product is that context, delivered with the data. The same foundation that ends your dashboard disagreements is what makes your S/4HANA data trustworthy enough to hand to an AI.

So the shift from pipelines to products is really two shifts at once. It retires a decade of brittle, unowned extractions and their hidden maintenance cost. And it builds the governed, meaningful foundation the next wave of enterprise AI actually requires, data that is AI-ready because it is governed and meaningful, not merely moved faster.

That is what changes when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.

SAP Enterprise Architecture Designer SAP Datasphere SAP Databricks SAP Analytics Cloud, connectivity SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: Update CCM version does not update mapping and range tables
entry.content:
Hello, all!

A problem we've faced up until the 2023 release is that the CCM version update change process does not update mapping and range tables. If CC creates a new chargeplan for a subscription product and we need to do a new cross catalog mapping in SOM, when we update the CCM and the contract is versioned, the mapping and range tables are erased. We would need to create a new change order for all the contracts updated and manually assign and fill all the tables again.

Has this been solved in newer versions? Are there workarounds to this? On the same note, do we have any standard methods for mass updates of MTs and RTs?

Thanks for the help!

entry.title: Re: how can i add an image and reset password date in the...
entry.content:
Hello @Ritumbhara ,

"1. Is there a way where i can add a logo or an image to my email template in cdc email template. I tried couple of things, but nothing worked."
As one of the options, you can add <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/59/SAP_2011_logo.svg" width="200"> somewhere in your template.



"2. I also want to add when the password reset happened(date). I looked for an attribute at profile level. But i couldn't find any attribute which holds the reset password date."
Unfortunately, the variables available in email templates are predefined by CDC and vary by template.

Email placeholders:
$firstName- The user's first name
$lastName - The user's last name
$nickname - The user's nickname
$username - The user's name as defined in the loginIDs.username field in the Account object
$name - The user's name
$birthDay - The day on which the user was born
$birthMonth - The month in which the user was born
$birthYear - The year in which the user was born
$age - The user's age
$UID - The user's UID
$email - The user's email address (not supported for DOI emails, Double Opt-In email templates must use $profile.email)
$gender - The user's gender
$city - The user's city
$state - The user's state
$zip - The user's postal code
$country - The user's country
$photoURL - A url pointing to the user's profile photo
$thumbnailURL - A url pointing to a thumbnail of the user's profile photo
$profileURL - A url pointing to the user's profile

Password Reset Email placeholders:
$pwResetURL - the reset page URL as defined in the policy (see above).
$pwResetToken - this is the token that should be passed as the passwordResetToken parameter of the accounts.resetPassword API call.
$pwResetLink - By default, this is composed of $pwResetURL?pwrt=$pwResetToken

But I see one possible workaround
1. Define one not used OOTB Email Placeholder in your implementation (for example, $name (profile.name), $gender (profile.gender), etc)
2. Create a dataflow where you replicate the value password.created as a string to this profile field.
3. Use it in the email template

entry.title: Re: Issue in standard Internal order report kob1 and kob1...
entry.content:
Hi Haldiram,

This is actually standard system behavior — KOB1/KOB1N and the trial balance are measuring two different things, so it's expected that they show different amounts.

Why KOB1 shows the CWIP G/L

KOB1 displays CO line items stored in table COVP/ACDOCA. For each line item, it shows the offsetting account (field GKONT) — the G/L account that was on the other side of the original FI document when the cost was posted to the Internal Order. So if your CWIP account appeared as the credit when costs were charged to the IO, KOB1 will show CWIP as the offsetting account for those line items.

Why the trial balance shows zero

The trial balance shows the net FI balance of the CWIP G/L account — all debits minus all credits across all postings. A zero balance simply means those postings have been cleared or offset (for example, when costs were settled out of the IO or capitalized to an asset, the CWIP account would have been credited/debited to bring it back to zero)

These two questions have different answers by design. The individual CO line items referencing CWIP still exist in COVP/ACDOCA even after the FI balance nets to zero.

This behavior is documented in SAP Note 39955 ("Offsetting account in CO document (apparently) incorrect"), which explains the offsetting account logic in CO documents.

Hope this helps clarify!

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: T811s Table currency field (FACURR) deriving details.
entry.content:
Hello Vittal,

1. How is FACURR derived in T811S?

Table T811S stores the cycle segment definition for CO allocation cycles (assessment, distribution, periodic reposting). The field FACURR is populated when you configure a cycle segment in KSU1/KSU2/KSU3 (or the equivalent transactions for other cycle types).

When the sender rule is set to "Fixed amounts" or the receiver rule uses a "Fixed rate", the system requires you to enter a currency directly in the segment configuration. That entered value is what gets stored in T811S-FACURR. For all other sender rules (including "Posted amounts"), the system may still store a technical default in FACURR (typically the Controlling Area currency), but this value is not functionally used.

2. Why is the currency field not visible for "Posted amounts" sender rule?

This is by design.

The SAP Help Portal (S/4HANA On-Premise 2025, Segments page) describes the assessment sender rules as:

"Posted amounts (rule 1) / Fixed portions (rule 2) / Fixed rates (rule 3). If you use rule 2 or 3, you have to enter the fixed portions or rates for the individual senders on the Sender Values tab."

Notice rule 1 (Posted amounts) has no such requirement. When you use Posted amounts, the system reads the actual cost line items from COVP/ACDOCA at the time the cycle runs — those documents already carry their own amounts and currencies. You'll only see it when you choose Fixed amounts or a Fixed rate, because those are the only cases where you're defining a specific amount in a specific currency ahead of time.

— SAP Help Portal, Segments (SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2025 > CO-PA > Periodic Overhead Allocation)

3. Impact of changing FACURR in T811S from USD to GBP for a "Posted amounts" segment

Short answer: no impact on cycle execution, but the change is not recommended.

SAP Note 499305 ("ALLOCATION: Segment currency appears in cycle print", component CO-OM-CCA-F) states explicitly:

"The currency does not have any influence on the execution of the cycle, as long as you do not use rule 'Fixed amounts'."

So for a segment using "Posted amounts", even if you directly change FACURR from USD to GBP in the table:

  • The cycle will not allocate in GBP
  • No currency conversion will occur
  • The cycle execution itself will not error out

However, it can cause a inconsistency — the incorrect currency may appear on the cycle print output (this is exactly the scenario Note 499305 describes as a known side effect). More importantly, direct table modification of T811S is not recommended. Cycle configuration should only be maintained through the standard transactions (KSU1/KSU2/KSU3, KSV1/KSW1, etc.).

For "Posted amounts" cycles, the allocation amounts are always derived from the actual posted line items in COVP/ACDOCA — FACURR plays no role at runtime regardless of its value.

Hope this helps!

Best regards,

entry.title: "Your AI kitchen is ready. Here's why you're not cooking yet."
entry.content:
Introduction

Think of your AI journey like building a restaurant. You don't just open the doors and start cooking. There is a process. And every organization that skipped that process learned the hard way — usually in front of a paying customer.

If you are working with SAP Customer Experience solutions — Commerce, Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, or SAP Engagement Cloud — you have probably already heard that AI is built right into the platform. And that is true. The capabilities are there. Joule is ready. Embedded AI is waiting to be switched on. But here is what nobody tells you clearly enough: having the kitchen does not mean you are ready to cook.

"This isn't twelve random services. It's one journey with four stages. You start at Discover, and every service has a clear reason to exist in that sequence."

In this blog post, you will walk through the twelve AI services that make up the SAP LOB Advanced Success Plan AI Service Catalog — what they are, why they are sequenced the way they are, and what they specifically mean for your SAP CX investment. The restaurant analogy will guide you the whole way, because once you see it, you cannot unsee it.

Background

The state of AI in SAP Customer Experience

SAP Customer Experience is not short on AI. Product recommendations in Commerce. Intelligent case routing in Service Cloud. Predictive lead scoring in Sales Cloud. AI-generated audience segments in Marketing. And Joule — SAP's generative AI copilot — running across all of them.

The honest conversation that needs to happen more often is this: many customers have already licensed these capabilities and have not yet turned them on. Not because they do not want to. Because they do not know where to start, or they started in the wrong place and it did not go well.

Why AI adoption keeps failing — three patterns worth knowing

Across enterprise CX deployments, the same three failure patterns appear again and again:

  • Starting blind. Activating AI without business context and Process Knowledge  — leading to duplicated effort and missed quick wins.
  • Skipping governance. Deploying tools without putting governance, ownership, or trust guardrails in place first — then being surprised when AI outputs cannot be audited or explained.
  • Forgetting the humans. Going live technically while the people who are supposed to use the AI were never prepared — and then measuring low adoption rates six months later and blaming the technology.

None of these are technology problems. They are journey problems. And that is exactly what the SAP CX AI Service Catalog was designed to solve.

Four phases. Twelve services. One kitchen.

The framework is built around four phases and twelve services. Each phase has a clear purpose. Each service has a clear reason to exist in its position in the sequence. Here is how it all maps to the restaurant analogy — and to your SAP CX reality.

Phase 1 — DISCOVER: First, understand the kitchen

"What ingredients do you have? What's already on the menu? That's AI Foundation Orientation."

Service 1 — AI Foundation Orientation

"Understand the kitchen, the ingredients, and what is already on the menu."

Before you plan a single AI initiative, you need to know your starting point. This service gives your team a structured level-set on SAP Business AI, the AI Foundation architecture, and Joule — so that every decision made after this point is grounded in what you actually have, not what you think you have.

Service 2 — AI Use Case Navigator

"Pick the dishes customers actually want before stocking the premium ingredients."

A restaurant that tries to serve everything has a menu nobody can read. This service uses live demos of SAP CX AI scenarios and a facilitated prioritization process to produce a shortlist of high-value use cases specific to your business — whether that is AI-assisted case resolution, next-best-offer in Commerce, or intelligent lead scoring in Sales Cloud. You invest in what your customers will actually value.

Phase 2 — PLAN/EXPLORE: Set the kitchen up to run well

"And you set up food safety — AI Trust & Compliance. Not exciting, but try running a restaurant without it."

Service 3 — AI Trust & Compliance

"Just like food safety keeps a restaurant trustworthy, AI governance keeps AI safe, compliant, and reliable."

AI that interacts with your customers carries real risk: biased recommendations, privacy violations, responses that cannot be explained or audited. This service establishes the guardrails — GDPR compliance, model transparency, output auditability — before a single customer sees an AI-generated result. Not optional. Not something to revisit after go-live.

Service 4 — AI CoE Foundation

"Build the head-chef team, kitchen rules, and recipe governance."

The best kitchen in the world fails without clear ownership and a chain of command. AI CoE Foundation helps your organization build the internal Center of Excellence — the team structure, governance model, and capability-building framework that means AI adoption does not collapse when the external experts leave.

Service 5 — AI Change Enablement

"Train the staff so the smart kitchen runs smoothly."

Your service agents, sales reps, and marketing managers are the ones who will use the AI every day. If they do not trust it, do not understand it, or do not know when to override it, adoption will stall regardless of how well the technology was deployed. This service prepares your people before go-live — not after the adoption numbers come in disappointing.

Phase 3 — REALIZE: Now you cook

"Now you cook. Joule Activation gets the core system connected. AI Agent Activation gives the digital sous-chefs their jobs. Embedded AI blends intelligence directly into the process — like seasoning in the sauce."

Five services. The kitchen powers on.

Service 6 — AI Activation Readiness Session

"Make sure the kitchen, tools, and suppliers are ready before opening."

A pre-deployment technical check confirming data quality, integration health, and configuration readiness across your in-scope CX environment. This is where you catch problems before they become customer-facing incidents — not after.

Service 7 — Joule Activation Session

"Start connecting the core systems and activate it."

The moment Joule powers on across your CX tools. Platform integration, user provisioning, and core activation — done correctly from day one. Your service agents, sales team, and commerce managers start interacting with AI where they already work.

Service 8 — Embedded AI Activation

"Blend AI directly into the cooking process, like seasoning in the sauce."

Product recommendations. Churn scores. Case routing. Sentiment-triggered escalation. These run invisibly inside your CX processes — but only if each scenario is correctly configured and validated. This service confirms every in-scope embedded AI capability is producing reliable outputs before it touches a real customer interaction.

Service 9 — AI Agent Activation

"Give the digital sous-chefs clear jobs and proper supervision. One at a time."

AI agents handle real customer interactions — resolving tickets, guiding self-service journeys. This service activates them with defined scope, oversight controls, and human-handoff protocols. Sequenced deliberately: one agent at a time, until each one is running with precision.

Phase 4 — RUN/OPERATE: Review, optimize, expand

"And then you keep optimizing. What's selling? What's not? Where do you expand? That's AI Operational Excellence."

Service 10 — AI Process Fit-Gap Analysis

"See where the standard menu works and where you need a custom special."

Not every customer's CX process maps cleanly to SAP's standard AI capabilities. This service identifies exactly where the gaps are and recommends whether the answer is configuration, extension, or a custom build — preventing months of effort spent on the wrong solution.

Service 11 — AI Operational Excellence

"Review what's selling, what's working, and where to expand the franchise."

The restaurant is open. Now the work is continuous improvement. This service reviews AI adoption metrics and value realization against original targets — is case deflection in Service Cloud hitting the forecast? Are Commerce recommendations lifting basket value? — and builds the roadmap for the next wave of AI investment.

Service 12 — Joule Studio Build

"Create custom signature dishes when the standard menu is not enough."

When SAP's out-of-the-box AI does not fully address a specific CX scenario, this service provides guided support to develop custom AI extensions — responsibly, within clean core principles, so your system stays upgradable while your AI stays differentiated.

Running throughout all four phases: Success Expert Services

Service Planning, Adoption Checkpoint, and Innovation Checkpoint run continuously across the entire journey — not as occasional project check-ins, but as the coordination layer that ensures every service lands in the right sequence, adoption milestones are being met alongside technical progress, and your team is always informed about what is new in SAP CX AI.

Think of them as the maître d' of your AI journey. Always present. Never optional.

 Results — what the journey actually delivers

Here is what customers who follow this sequence consistently experience — phase by phase:

After Discover:

  • A shared, documented picture of your current AI estate — no more guessing what is active.
  • A prioritized use case shortlist that business and IT have both agreed on.
  • Alignment between teams on where the journey starts and why.

After Plan/Explore:

  • A governance structure and CoE that will outlast the project.
  • A compliance framework that satisfies audit requirements before go-live.
  • A trained, AI-ready user population — prepared before the technology arrives.

After Realize:

  • Joule activated and in use across the CX tools your teams already work in.
  • Embedded AI scenarios validated against real data before touching a customer interaction.
  • AI agents running with defined scope, oversight, and human-handoff protocols.
  • Gaps between standard capability and process requirements documented and resolved.

After Run/Operate:

  • Closed-loop value measurement — you know what your AI investment actually delivered.
  • A data-backed roadmap for the next wave of AI in your CX landscape.
  • Custom capabilities where needed, built safely within clean core principles.

The difference between organizations that reach sustained AI value and those that do not is rarely the quality of the technology. It is almost always the quality of the journey around it.

 Conclusion and what comes next

This is not twelve random services. It is one journey with four stages. You start at Discover, and every service has a clear reason to exist in that sequence.

The restaurant analogy works because the adoption challenge is the same: you cannot serve a great meal without first knowing your kitchen, picking the right menu, training your team, and then continuously reviewing what is working. Organizations that treat AI adoption as this kind of structured journey — from Foundation Orientation through to Operational Excellence — reach value faster, sustain adoption longer, and build the organizational capability to scale AI across their entire customer experience.

Where the journey goes next

The field is evolving quickly. A few directions worth watching as this methodology matures:

  • Agentic AI at scale — as multi-agent orchestration matures in SAP CX, the framework will need to govern agent-to-agent interactions across Commerce, Sales, and Service simultaneously.
  • Industry-specific tracks — future iterations of this catalog should develop industry-specific variants for retail, utilities, and financial services CX scenarios.
  • Value measurement standards — standardized benchmarks for CX AI value — deflection rates, basket lift, pipeline conversion — will allow organizations to evaluate outcomes against a curated reference dataset rather than self-defined metrics.
  • Customer-led innovation pathways — formal routes for customer-originated CX AI use cases to be evaluated and contributed back into SAP's standard capability roadmap.

Great AI. Even better service.

Sustained AI value is built on trust, adoption, and continuous improvement

Where are you on this journey? Have you already started with AI Foundation Orientation, or are you still figuring out where to begin? I would love to hear what is working for your team and what questions are still open.

Share your thoughts in the comments below — and if you found this post useful, explore the SAP Customer Experience topic page on SAP Community for more expert content on AI adoption, Joule, and the Advanced Success Plan.

entry.title: Re: UD Reversal at Warehouse
entry.content:
Hi customer,

Thank you for your question.

It is not possible to reverse usage decision stock posting for EWM inspection lot (origin:17). Although for non-EWM inspection lots, this can be done by the modification solution included in SAP Note 175842 (report RQEVAC50), it cannot be used in EWM environment. This is because in case of EWM inspection lots, all physical movement occurs in EWM via the follow-up action of the usage decision, which is not reversible.

However, KBA 2551912 describes approaches for adjusting the stock at EWM side.

 

Best regards,
Lily

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 Is Here: What It Means for Your Upgrade Journey
entry.content:
Announcing the Release of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022

As outlined in SAP Note 3424346, SAP has released SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022.

Although this is not a new SAP product, it represents an important milestone for customers running SAP S/4HANA 2022. Safekeeper is released for specific SAP S/4HANA innovation releases to help eligible customers continue operating their existing release after the end of mainstream maintenance, while they plan and execute an upgrade to a newer release under mainstream maintenance.

The availability of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 gives customers additional flexibility to remain on SAP S/4HANA 2022 for a limited period while preparing their transition to a more recent SAP S/4HANA release.

Why is there a three-year gap before the next Safekeeper release?

One question customers may notice is why the next planned Safekeeper release—for SAP S/4HANA 2023—is expected only three years later.

The answer lies in SAP's updated SAP S/4HANA release strategy, introduced in 2022.

Beginning with SAP S/4HANA 2023, SAP implemented several significant lifecycle improvements:

  • A two-year innovation release cycle, replacing the previous annual cadence.

  • Seven years of mainstream maintenance for each innovation release, extended from the previous five-year period.

  • Feature Package Stacks (FPS) that deliver continuous innovation in smaller, easier-to-adopt increments without requiring customers to move immediately to the next innovation release.

These changes provide customers with longer planning horizons, greater operational stability, and more predictable upgrade cycles. Consequently, lifecycle milestones—including future Safekeeper releases—are now naturally spaced further apart.

What does this mean for SAP S/4HANA 2022 customers?

For organizations currently running SAP S/4HANA 2022, the release of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 provides additional flexibility when planning their application lifecycle.

Customers who need more time beyond mainstream maintenance to complete testing, business validation, or transformation initiatives can leverage Safekeeper (subject to the applicable commercial terms and eligibility criteria) while preparing for their upgrade.

However, Safekeeper should be viewed as a bridge—not a destination. Customers are encouraged to define their upgrade roadmap early so they can benefit from the innovations, security enhancements, and longer maintenance horizons available in newer SAP S/4HANA releases.

Why SAP S/4HANA 2023 and 2025 deserve serious consideration

Customers upgrading from SAP S/4HANA 2022 will almost certainly target either SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA 2025.

These releases deliver far more than a longer maintenance timeline.

SAP S/4HANA 2023: The beginning of SAP's next-generation ERP lifecycle

SAP S/4HANA 2023 is the first release built on SAP's new lifecycle strategy, providing seven years of mainstream maintenance and a more predictable two-year innovation cadence.

It also marks the beginning of the Business AI era for SAP S/4HANA. For eligible SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition deployments, SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 introduces support for Joule, SAP's generative AI copilot. Users can interact with business applications using natural language, receive contextual assistance, and streamline navigation across business processes.

Combined with continuous innovation delivered through Feature Package Stacks, SAP S/4HANA 2023 establishes a strong long-term platform for customers modernizing their ERP landscape.

SAP S/4HANA 2025: Building on the foundation

SAP S/4HANA 2025 builds on the 2023 foundation with broader Business AI capabilities and further enhancements across the application.

Highlights include:

  • Expanded Joule integration and additional AI-powered business scenarios.

  • Enhanced Fiori user experience with refined applications and embedded insights.

  • Stronger Clean Core support through additional released APIs, business events, and extensibility options.

  • Functional enhancements across Finance, Supply Chain, Manufacturing, Asset Management, Sustainability, and other business areas.

  • A mainstream maintenance horizon extending to approximately 2032, providing customers with an even longer period of lifecycle stability.

For organizations beginning a new implementation or planning a major upgrade over the next few years, SAP S/4HANA 2025 is likely to be the preferred target release, balancing innovation, stability, and long-term support.

Final thoughts

The release of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 provides customers with valuable flexibility, allowing additional time to prepare for their next SAP S/4HANA upgrade where needed.

At the same time, it reinforces the importance of proactive lifecycle planning. Whether the destination is SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA 2025, customers have an opportunity to move beyond simply remaining supported and instead take advantage of SAP's latest innovations in Business AI, Clean Core, user experience, and continuous functional enhancements.

Safekeeper helps extend the runway—but the real value lies in reaching the next generation of SAP S/4HANA.

entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am trying to create a freestyle SAPUI5 application in SAP Build Code (BAS) using the new Joule command:
/ui5-create-app

Prompt used

/ui5-create-app Create a freestyle SAPUI5 application for the Manage Sales Order CAP service.
Use the OData V4 service:
/service/manage_Sales_Order_App

CAP Project Information
I already have a CAP project generated successfully using Joule.

db/
srv/
app/
The OData service is available and generated correctly.

Joule Response
Joule returns the following proposal:
Let's configure your Manage Sales Order app using JavaScript instead of TypeScript.
Namespace: com.myorg.managesalesorder
Base Path:
/home/user/projects/Manage_Sales_Order_App/app
Create App Directory: true
OData V4 URL:
service/manage_Sales_Order_App/
OData Entity Set:
SalesOrders
TypeScript: false

by pressing the "Accept Application" button.
Issue

Failed to generate app proposal:

Cannot find package '@ui5/mcp-server'
imported from

/extbin/local/openvscode-server/extensions/
sap-ux-help-extension-1.28.0/dist/extension-min.cjs

Did you mean to import
"@ui5/mcp-server/lib/api.js"?

SAP Fiori Tools - Guided Development

Troubleshooting Performed

Refreshing BAS browser window
Opening a new Joule conversation
Using a simplified /ui5-create-app prompt
Creating a new Development Space
Reopening the project

The same error occurs every time.

Environment
SAP Build Code / SAP Business Application Studio
SAP Fiori Tools - Guided Development
Extension version reported in error:
sap-ux-help-extension-1.28.0
Questions
Is this a known issue with the SAP Fiori Tools / UI5 Create Application assistant?
Has anyone encountered the missing package error:
@ui5/mcp-server
Is there any workaround available until the issue is fixed?
Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Migrate from SAP Neo, Cloud Integration to SAP Integration Suite with Confidence
entry.content:
Migrating from SAP Cloud Integration in the Neo environment to SAP Integration Suite on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry is more than a technical migration - it's an opportunity to align with SAP's strategic multi-cloud foundation and take advantage of modern integration capabilities.

To help you navigate this transition, we've published a comprehensive guide that brings together SAP documentation, best practices, and SAP Enterprise Support services to support every stage of your migration journey.

What you'll learn

This guide walks you through the migration using the SAP Activate methodology, covering every phase from planning to post-go-live optimization:

  • Discover – Understand why SAP BTP Cloud Foundry is the strategic direction and explore the benefits of SAP Integration Suite.
  • Prepare – Assess your existing integration landscape and learn how the free Neo Sunset Migration Factory can help you create a migration roadmap.
  • Explore – Discover onboarding resources, hands-on workshops, consulting services, and best practices to accelerate your migration.
  • Realize & Deploy – Learn about SAP migration tools, Postman collections, testing strategies, and cutover recommendations for a successful go-live.
  • Run – Optimize and monitor your integration landscape with SAP Cloud ALM, Continuous Quality Checks (CQC), Cloud Application Services (CAS), and Expert-Guided Implementations.

Why read this guide?

Whether you're just starting your migration planning or already executing your project, this article provides a single place to discover:

  • Official SAP migration resources
  • Available SAP Enterprise Support services
  • Adoption and enablement offerings
  • SAP Consulting services
  • Best practices for reducing migration risk and accelerating time-to-value

By leveraging the right guidance and support services, organizations can confidently transition to SAP's strategic multi-cloud foundation while minimizing business disruption.

If you're preparing for your migration to SAP Integration Suite on Cloud Foundry, this guide is an excellent starting point.

Looking for detailed migration steps?

As part of this guide, you'll also find a link to our companion article, "SAP Neo, Cloud Integration to SAP Integration Suite Migration Guide." This supplementary guide provides additional insight into the migration process with step-by-step instructions, screenshots, and clearly structured sections, making it an excellent reference when you're ready to execute the migration of your integration scenarios.

Together, these resources provide both the strategic guidance and the practical information needed to help you migrate with confidence.

👉Read the full guide "How to Migrate from SAP Neo, Cloud Integration to SAP Integration Suite through Support Services" on SAP Business Technology Platform Value Map and discover how SAP can support you throughout your migration journey. To access Value Map Groups, you need to login to the SAP Community using an S-user ID or a Universal ID.

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps  

Beyond this topic, SAP Enterprise Support value maps on SAP Community provides guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next—all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉 Watch short demo video, then head over to our page - explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.   

About the author:  

Carol Zhang is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). With more than 20 years of experience at SAP and a background spanning roles in SAP Service and Support organization, she is passionate about helping customers succeed businesses accelerate the adoption of SAP BTP tools and services through SAP Enterprise Support offerings. 

💡Have questions or feedback around this content? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @CarolZhang.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | My Inbox App
entry.content:
Hi @quocdt5 ,

I will get right to your two questions:

 

1) The business catalog SAP_CA_BC_MYINBOX_PC has 8 dependent business catalogs. Is it recommended to include all of these dependent catalogs in my custom business role, or should I only add them when a specific business scenario requires them?

In our approval role, we only included the dependent catalogs which we specifically need. For example, at the moment we are not working with Service Entry Sheets → Business catalog SAP_MM_BC_SES_WIAPPROVE_PC is not included in our approval role.

 

2) I would like the workflow manager to manage workflow tasks only for a specific company code, instead of all workflow tasks in the system. Is it possible to restrict My Inbox or workflow task management by Company Code?

The business catalog SAP_CA_BC_MYINBOX_PC does not contain a restriction check for company code, in fact it does not contain any restrictions:

Therefore, all restrictions that you want to use have to come from another business catalog that is used in the same role.

When you check the dependent business catalog for Supplier Invoice Approval, company code is available as a restriction.

→  So, by combining the catalogs for My Inbox and for your specific approval scenarios you should be able to achieve your goal.

 

Best regards,

Kerisha

 

Kerisha Lawrence

Public Cloud | IAM, Procurement, Post Go-Live

https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerisha-lawrence
