entry.title: Keystore Certificate Expiration Alerts in SAP Integration Suite - Part 2
entry.content:
Introduction

Managing certificates is a responsibility for SAP Integration support team. Certificates stored in the Keystore are used to establish secure communication with external systems. If a certificate expires without notice, it can cause integration failures and business disruptions.

To avoid this, organizations often rely on manual monitoring, which is time-consuming and prone to oversight.

In this blog, I would like to explain- how to build an automated monitoring solution in SAP Integration Suite:

  • Retrieves all certificates from the tenant Keystore using the OData API.
  • Filters certificates approaching expiration using a Groovy script.
  • Converts the filtered data into a well-formatted HTML table using XSLT.
  • Sends an email notification automatically through the Mail Adapter.

CPI Design:

The integration consists of the following steps:

Step

Description

Timer

To start the Integration once a day

ContentModifier

Externalized properties such as sender, receiver email addresses and selection of days to check in expiry

Request Reply

Calls the CI OData API to retrieve Keystore certificates.

Filter Expired Certificates

Uses a Groovy script to identify certificates expiring within the configured notification period.

Router

Check if there are records in the body.  Continue If yes. End the message if not.

XSLT Mapping

Transform the XML response into an HTML table.

Mail Adapter

To Send the formatted HTML email notification.

ContentModifier

Prepares the xml Body incase of an error

Send with Mail Adapter

Send the error info to the operation teams  in case of an error

 Timer:
A simple time schedule to run once a day.
Content Modifier:
It stores the sender and receiver email addresses as exchange properties. These properties are later referenced in the Mail Adapter configuration, making the integration easier to maintain.

Configure the OData Receiver Adapter: 
The OData Receiver Adapter is used to retrieve all certificate entries from the SAP Integration Suite Keystore using the Integration Suite OData API.

The API returns details such as:

  • Certificate Alias
  • Key Type
  • Certificate Status
  • Certificate Expiration Date (ValidNotAfter)

Some Odata API's allows you to filter the records but for this KeyStore API entity, it is not a choice. So, the oData results are filtered later by the Groovy script. I have created an additional blog for consuming & testing the OData Services, please check out here if you need any assistance.

Test Odata service using Postman 

 

The integration invokes the SAP Integration Suite OData API to retrieve all certificates stored in the tenant's Keystore. A sample response looks like this:

<KeystoreEntries>
   <KeystoreEntry>
      <Alias>smtp.gmail.com</Alias>
      <KeyType>EC</KeyType>
      <ValidNotAfter>2026-08-10T18:37:12.000</ValidNotAfter>
      <Status>unchanged</Status>
   </KeystoreEntry>
</KeystoreEntries>

I have created another blog post for how to connect security content odata api using postman in-detail.  in this blog

Filter Certificates xml data Using Groovy

Instead of implementing complex date calculations in graphical message mapping, I chose Groovy. The script compares the current date (+7 or +14days as needed) with the ValidNotAfter field and keeps only certificates that are due to expire within the  configured notification window (for example, the next 14 days).

This approach is simpler, easier to maintain, and provides greater flexibility when implementing date-based logic.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.xml.XmlUtil

def Message processData(Message message) {
	def body = message.getBody(String)
	def xml = new XmlParser().parseText(body)
	
	Date today = new Date()
	Date threshold = today + 14
	
	xml.KeystoreEntry.findAll { cert ->
	Date expiry = Date.parse("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS", cert.ValidNotAfter.text() )
	expiry <= threshold
	}.each { }

	xml.KeystoreEntry.findAll { cert ->
	Date expiry = Date.parse("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS", cert.ValidNotAfter.text())
	!(expiry <= threshold)
	}.each { it.parent().remove(it) }

	message.setBody(XmlUtil.serialize(xml))
return message
}
Router Configuration

Since certificate expiration is not a daily occurrence, there is no need to send an email every time the integration flow runs. Instead, a Router step is used to determine whether any certificates meet the alert criteria.

I configured the Router to evaluate the groovy output step. If one or more certificates are approaching their expiration date, the message is routed to the Mail Receiver Adapter to send an email notification. Otherwise, the flow simply ends without sending any email.

Generate an HTML Table Using XSLT

The filtered XML is transformed into an HTML table using XSLT. Inline HTML styles are used to ensure the table is displayed correctly in email clients such as Outlook and Gmail.

The generated email contains:

  • Certificate Name
  • Key Type
  • Valid Until
  • Status
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet version="1.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">
	<xsl:output method="html" encoding="UTF-8" indent="yes"/>
	<xsl:template match="/">
		<html>
			<body style="font-family:Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif;font-size:13px;">
				<xsl:choose>
					<xsl:when test="count(/KeystoreEntries/KeystoreEntry) > 0">
						<h2>Certificates Expiring Soon</h2>
						<table border="1" cellpadding="6" cellspacing="0" width="100%" style="border-collapse:collapse;border:1px solid #000000;">
							<tr bgcolor="#D9D9D9">
								<th style="border:1px solid #000000;text-align:left;">Certificate Name</th>
								<th style="border:1px solid #000000;text-align:left;">Key Type</th>
								<th style="border:1px solid #000000;text-align:left;">Valid Until</th>
								<th style="border:1px solid #000000;text-align:left;">Status</th>
							</tr>
							<xsl:for-each select="/KeystoreEntries/KeystoreEntry">
								<tr>
									<td style="border:1px solid #000000;">
										<xsl:value-of select="Alias"/>
									</td>
									<td style="border:1px solid #000000;">
										<xsl:value-of select="KeyType"/>
									</td>
									<td style="border:1px solid #000000;">
										<xsl:value-of select="ValidNotAfter"/>
									</td>
									<td style="border:1px solid #000000;">
										<xsl:value-of select="Status"/>
									</td>
								</tr>
							</xsl:for-each>
						</table>
					</xsl:when>
					<xsl:otherwise>
						<h2>No certificates found.</h2>
					</xsl:otherwise>
				</xsl:choose>
			</body>
		</html>
	</xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>

 Mail Adapter Configuration :
  To ensure the HTML content is rendered correctly by email clients, configure the following settings in the Mail Adapter:

Mime Type: Text/HTML
Content Encoding: Quoted-Printable

These settings instruct the mail client to interpret the message body as HTML rather than plain text. As a result, the certificate information is displayed in a well-formatted table instead of showing the raw HTML tags.

Another blog for explaining how to create Gmail app password, please check out here if you need any assistance.
Gmail SMTP Integration 

Email notification: 
After the integration flow is executed successfully, an email notification is sent containing the list of certificates that meet the configured criteria (for example, certificates approaching their expiration date).

The HTML formatting makes the report easy to read, allowing administrators to quickly identify certificates that require attention without manually checking the SAP Integration Suite tenant.

Exception Handling

To improve the integration error handling, an Exception Subprocess is added. If an error occurs during the execution of the iFlow—such as an API connection issue, authentication failure, or processing error—the Exception Subprocess is triggered automatically.

The Exception Subprocess sends an email notification containing the error details to the configured recipients. This enables the team to quickly identify and resolve issues without manually monitoring the integration flow, helping ensure timely response to failures.

Content Modifier : Below is the body i used 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" ?>
<AlertNotification>
	<item>
		<Sender>SAP CPI</Sender>
		<Receiver>Mail</Receiver>
		<SenderInterface>QueryKeystoreEntries_Out</SenderInterface>
		<ReceiverInterface>AlertNotification_In</ReceiverInterface>
		<IFlowName>${camelId}</IFlowName>
		<Application_ID>${header.SAP_ApplicationId}</Application_ID>
		<Correlation_ID>${header.SAP_MplCorrelationId}</Correlation_ID>
		<Message_ID>${property.SAP_MessageProcessingLogID}</Message_ID>
		<StartedAt>${header.SAP_ApplicationStopTime}</StartedAt>
		<CurrentDateTime>${date:now:dd-MM-yyyy HH:mm:ss}</CurrentDateTime>
		<TenantName>${property.SystemName}</TenantName>
		<Error>${exception.message}</Error>
		<OverallStatus>${header.SAP_ApplicationOverallStatus}</OverallStatus>
		<LatestActionAt>${header.SAP_ApplicationStartTime}</LatestActionAt>
		<CreatedTimestamp>${property.CamelCreatedTimestamp}</CreatedTimestamp>
		<ExceptionCaught>${property.CamelExceptionCaught}</ExceptionCaught>
		<HttpResponseCode>${header.CamelHttpResponseCode}</HttpResponseCode>
		<severity>INFO</severity>
		<category>NOTIFICATION</category>
	</item>
</AlertNotification>



Benefits:

This solution provides several advantages:

  • Automated certificate monitoring.
  • Early notification before certificate expiration.
  • Reduced manual effort.
  • Configurable notification period.
  • Reusable design for other monitoring scenarios, for ex. you can report the message processing logs
  • Readability through HTML email formatting.

entry.title: Re: Condition contract (WCOCO) triggers error "Field KVGR...
entry.content: Please check if the field KVGR1 is available in the KOMK / KOMP structure, if yes map it on the access sequence level else you need to find the userexit - userexit_pricing_prepare_tkomk or userexit_pricing_prepare_tkomp to map this field from transactional level to KOMK/KOMP structure level using a simple abap code similar to how you would do it for Sales order / Invoice pricing.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere Master Data – Attributes, Texts, and Hierarchies
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere Master Data Hierarchy – End-to-End G/L Account Modeling for SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)

In this blog, we will explore how to build a complete Master Data hierarchy in SAP Datasphere from scratch and understand how it can be consumed in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for Seamless Planning.
The objective is to demonstrate the end-to-end process of designing a hierarchy, creating the required artifacts, assembling them into a reusable master data dimension, and finally exposing it for planning and analytics.

The following topics will be covered:

  • Building the foundational objects required for master data, including Replication Flows, Local Tables, and Graphical Views.
  • Understanding the source data and how different data sources are utilized in the hierarchy design.
  • Preparing and transforming the data required for hierarchy modeling.
  • Designing the hierarchy structure by creating the required hierarchy artifacts.
  • Combining all the artifacts into a reusable Dimension that can be consumed by transaction data.
  • Consuming the hierarchy in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for Seamless Planning.

One of the key objectives of this blog is to highlight how hierarchy consumption for SAP Analytics Cloud Seamless Planning differs from the standard modeling approach in SAP Datasphere Analytical Models. While Analytical Models typically consume hierarchies using associations and hierarchy semantics, Seamless Planning follows a different modeling approach to ensure that SAC correctly recognizes and consumes the hierarchy.

By the end of this blog, you will have a clear understanding of the complete hierarchy modeling process in SAP Datasphere for Seamless Planning, from data replication to hierarchy consumption in SAP Analytics Cloud.

In a follow-up blog, we will explore how the same hierarchy can be modeled and consumed in a SAP Datasphere Analytical Model, including the use of hierarchy semantics, associations, and best practices.




Step 1: Replicate the Source Data

The same hierarchy modeling approach can be applied to any master data dimension, such as G/L Account, Customer, Product, Cost Center, Profit Center, or any other business master data object. The overall implementation process remains the same.

In this blog, we will demonstrate the complete end-to-end implementation using the G/L Account master data scenario.

Prerequisites

Before starting the hierarchy modeling process, ensure that the following source objects are available in SAP Datasphere:

  • G/L Account Master Data
  • G/L Account Text
  • Hierarchy Node
  • Hierarchy Node Text

The objective is to build a reusable hierarchy dimension that can be consumed by transactional data for reporting and planning scenarios.

The first step is to replicate all the required source objects into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows.

After the replication is complete, we will create the required local modeling artifacts and gradually assemble the hierarchy objects that will finally be consumed in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC).

Step 2: Build the Hierarchy Views

After replicating the source data, we will build the hierarchy using the following architecture.

The solution consists of two graphical views:

  • Graphical View – G/L Hierarchy
  • Graphical View – G/L Account with Text

These two views together provide the complete hierarchy structure and the corresponding master data attributes.

 

Graphical View – G/L Hierarchy

This view contains the hierarchy relationships, we did union of hierarchy node and GL account views.
semantic type - Dimension



Graphical view hierarchy node with text
semantic type - Dimension

Graphical view G/L account with text
semantic type – Dimension

 

Field

Purpose

GLAccountHierarchy

Hierarchy Identifier

HierarchyNode

Unique Hierarchy Node

ParentNode

Parent-Child Relationship

NodeType

Identifies Root, Intermediate, or Leaf Node

GLAccount

Leaf-level G/L Account

HierarchyNodeText

Hierarchy Node Description

GLAccountName

G/L Account Description

ChartOfAccounts

Chart of Accounts


The hierarchy structure is as follows:

GLAccountHierarchy

│

├── HierarchyNode (Root)

│

├── HierarchyNode (Intermediate)

│

├── HierarchyNode (Leaf)

│      │

│      └── GLAccount

│

└── ParentNode

Once these fields are modeled, the hierarchy view is complete.

Expose the graphical view by enabling Expose for Consumption.
semantic type - Dimension

 

Step 3: Consume the Hierarchy in SAP Analytics Cloud

Once the view is exposed, navigate to the object and select OData.

Copy the generated OData Service URL.

 

In SAP Analytics Cloud, create an OAuth 2.0 Connection to SAP Datasphere and use the generated OData service URL as the data source.

After the connection is established, import the hierarchy into your SAC Planning model, where it can be used for reporting, planning, and hierarchical navigation.

Step 4: Load the Hierarchy into SAP Analytics Cloud

After creating the G/L Account hierarchy and exposing it through the OData service, the next step is to load the data into the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Planning Dimension using Data Management.

The hierarchy is loaded in three separate import queries.

Query 1 – Load G/L Account Master Data

The first query loads the G/L Account master data along with its corresponding descriptions (texts) into the G/L Account dimension.

This creates the base members of the dimension.

Query 2 – Load Hierarchy Nodes

The second query loads the Hierarchy Nodes and their corresponding Parent Nodes.

This step creates the hierarchical parent-child structure within the dimension.


Query 3 – Load Hierarchy Node Values

The final query loads the Hierarchy Node Value together with the Parent Node mapping.

The Hierarchy Node Value represents the leaf member (in this example, the G/L Account), while the Parent Node defines where the member belongs within the hierarchy.

In other words, the Parent Node establishes the hierarchy levels (Level 0, Level 1, Level 2, and so on) by linking each node to its immediate parent.

Once all three Data Management imports have been executed successfully, the complete G/L Account hierarchy is available in the SAC Planning model and can be used for reporting, planning, filtering, and hierarchical navigation.

Note: The hierarchy is built incrementally. The first import creates the master data members, the second import establishes the hierarchy nodes, and the third import links the leaf members to their respective parent nodes, resulting in the complete hierarchy structure.
We took Node type = L into filter.

Why is the hierarchy loaded in three separate Data Management imports?

At first glance, it may seem that the entire hierarchy could be loaded in a single import. However, SAP Analytics Cloud internally treats master data members, hierarchy nodes, and hierarchy relationships as different entities. Therefore, each of them must be loaded separately.

To understand this, consider the following hierarchy:

  

YCOA

│

└── Balance Sheet

    │

    ├── Assets

    │   ├── Fixed Assets

    │   │   ├── 150000 - Building

    │   │   └── 151000 - Machinery

    │   │

    │   └── Current Assets

    │       ├── 100000 - Cash

    │       └── 101000 - Bank

    │

    └── Liabilities

        └── 200000 - Loan

 

This hierarchy actually consists of two different object types.

The first type is the Hierarchy Nodes, which define the structure of the hierarchy.

Balance Sheet

Assets

Fixed Assets

Current Assets

Liabilities

These are organizational nodes. They do not contain transactional data and do not represent actual business objects. Their purpose is only to organize the hierarchy into different levels.

The second type is the Leaf Members.

100000 - Cash

101000 - Bank

150000 - Building

151000 - Machinery

200000 - Loan

These are the actual G/L Account members that store transactional data. Every posting in the fact table ultimately points to one of these leaf members.

Because hierarchy nodes and leaf members serve different purposes, SAP Analytics Cloud stores them separately.

Import 1 – Master Data

The first import creates only the leaf members.

100000 - Cash

101000 - Bank

150000 - Building

At this point, SAC knows these G/L Accounts exist, but it has no information about where they belong in the hierarchy.

Import 2 – Hierarchy Nodes

The second import creates the hierarchy structure.

Balance Sheet

    └── Assets

          ├── Fixed Assets

          └── Current Assets

Here, SAC creates only the hierarchy nodes and their parent-child relationships.

Notice that no G/L Accounts are assigned yet.

Import 3 – Hierarchy Node Values

The final import connects the two worlds.

150000  → Fixed Assets

100000  → Current Assets

200000  → Liabilities

This tells SAC that the leaf member 150000 belongs under the hierarchy node Fixed Assets, while 100000 belongs under Current Assets.

Only after this mapping does the complete hierarchy become available.

YCOA

└── Balance Sheet

    ├── Assets

    │   ├── Fixed Assets

    │   │   └── 150000 - Building

    │   │

    │   └── Current Assets

    │       ├── 100000 - Cash

    │       └── 101000 - Bank

    │

    └── Liabilities

        └── 200000 - Loan

Why doesn't SAC load everything in one import?

The reason is that hierarchy nodes are not dimension members.

A node such as Assets or Balance Sheet is simply a structural element used for navigation and aggregation. It does not receive postings or contain business data.

The leaf members, on the other hand, are actual dimension members (such as G/L Accounts) that receive transactional postings.

By separating these concepts, SAP Analytics Cloud allows the same hierarchy structure to be reused, modified, or extended independently of the underlying master data. It also supports multiple hierarchies over the same set of dimension members, making the model more flexible and easier to maintain.


In this blog, we built a complete G/L Account hierarchy in SAP Datasphere and exposed it for consumption in SAP Analytics Cloud. Although the modeling process is straightforward, understanding the hierarchy structure and selecting the correct fields is essential for successful hierarchy consumption in SAC Planning. In the next blog, we will explore how the same hierarchy can be consumed in a SAP Datasphere Analytical Model, where hierarchy semantics, associations, and consumption patterns differ from the approach used for SAC Seamless Planning.

entry.title: CMI's Playbook for Joule Integration: From One Document to Agentic AI
entry.content:
Joule is now natively grounded in over 400 CMI competitive assets (CompeteGPT) and over 4,500 customer references through ReferenceGPT via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. Integration to Joule involved a lot more than just the technical work - finding the right resources, connecting with the right people, weeks of waiting when things broke and we didn't know what caused it or how to fix it, a few "aha" moments when we finally did, lots of testing and re-testing, and learning the process as we went.

One big takeaway at the end? If we knew what we know now, we could have shipped this in 3 months instead of 5.

With SAP's direction toward the autonomous suite, we wanted to share our learnings and exchange best practices as we head into our next phase: bringing an autonomous CMI Agent to life. This super-agent orchestrates knowledge gathering across all our internal CMI AI sub-agents to answer the most complex questions, and we can leverage shared common knowledge across the suite.

  • If your team is looking to add your documents or AI chatbot into Joule, this blog walks you through the starting steps, key contacts, links to essential resources, AI best practices, and lessons learned along the way. When we started, we were one of the very first teams doing this and had no peer insights to lean on.
  • If you're already ahead of the curve or interested in integrating our CMI agent into your system, we'd love to connect and learn from you. Especially as we move toward building a CMI agent that brings our full account intelligence to outside systems via A2A and MCP.

A little context first

If you're in sales and marketing, you've probably worked with CMI - the team that gathers and delivers competitive, customer and market intelligence inside SAP. For decades, CMI has served as SAP's market intelligence hub, collecting real-time signals from competitors, customers, industry analysts and translating them into actionable account insights, battlecards and alerts for our sales teams to win deals

But here's the core challenge we've been wrestling with: How do we collect and deliver intelligence in the AI era to the right person, at the right moment, while the information is still hot?

Imagine this: a single place where your team could find all the inter-connected intelligence from multiple channels they need using natural language. No more digging through scattered channels to get the right information. No more bookmarking standalone AI solutions on your laptop. Something you can access on your mobile with one prompt!

Joule became our answer - a way to scale this capability and ensure the right insights reach the right people at exactly the right time. A user inside Joule can now ask questions on how to win against a competitor, find a relevant customer reference, or uncover a market signal and get an intelligent, up-to-date answer powered by CMI intelligence. Without ever leaving their workflow. Anywhere, anytime.

So - how did we do it?

Stage 1: One Document, One Entry Point

We began with a single "CMI FAQ" document with resource links and key contacts, ingested into Joule. It provided guidance on where to find competitive intelligence, account insights, market models, analyst relations, and customer references.

For about a month, it worked fine. As thousands of documents flooded into Joule, our single CMI document became a needle in an ever-growing haystack. It didn’t show up in top search results for a variety of user prompts as it had limited information.

If you're just starting out and have limited documents/pdfs, you can Add your documents to Joule as a self-service with no coding required.

AI Tips for Better Results:

  • Write documents in an AI-friendly format for better Joule search results
  • Include prompt instructions and sample prompts within your document
  • Repeat key terms so your content ranks higher in search results
  • Expand all abbreviations (use hyperlinks where helpful)

Screenshots: before and after one document ingestion to Joule                                  

 

Stage 2: Grounding 400+ Competitive intelligence assets to Joule

The next natural question was obvious: if we could ingest one document, why not ingest all of them - every PDF, every doc, directly into Joule? This required a more sustainable solution without adding documents manually to the Self-service tool,

For weeks, we discussed the pros and cons of two technically complex options. Then one day, the Joule team presented us with a third path we hadn't considered.

Here were our choices:

Option A: Migrate our content from our database to SharePoint or AWS S3 so could Joule use it for document grounding?

Option B: Chunk the documents ourselves and share embeddings in SAP HANA DB with Joule?

Option C: Could Joule build an automated data pipeline, store it internally via API, and ground it automatically?

We went with Option C. Together with the Joule team; we built a direct data pipeline to Joule from our CMI CompeteOne database using API integration. All our competitive intelligence battlecards, analyst reports, CMI alerts are ingested in Joule every 24 hours.

Ready to get started? Check out the Fast Track Document Grounded Use Case Intake Guide to begin your integration.

Key Lessons: 

  • Talk to the Joule team early. Discuss your use-case upfront and partner with their technical team from day one. It saves months of rework. Also, dig into internal success stories on the One Joule SharePoint - you'll learn what works.
  • Prioritize business user testing. Get real users involved early to validate that Joule returns the right answers for real-world questions, this could inform how you write your documents to be AI-searchable.
  • Know the tracking gaps. Currently there is no usage of KPI tracking for grounded documents on the Joule dashboard.

Stage 3: Agentic Integration of our custom AI ReferenceGPT into Joule

The most ambitious part of our journey came next: bringing ReferenceGPT - our AI custom chatbot for finding the right SAP reference customers and their success stories into Joule as an agent via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. This is where things got truly intelligent.

Instead of Joule simply retrieving a document, it could now hand off a request to ReferenceGPT- a specialized agent built specifically for this job and bring the answer back into the conversation. Two agents, working together.

Why A2A instead of native ingestion?

ReferenceGPT isn't just another set of documents. It's a specialized code-based agent that interacts with multiple data channels - customer success stories on digital library, customer references profiles stored on refHub on MXP platform as structured & nested tables, and account attributes from CRM. It has an in-built custom code logic to translate text to SQL to pull the most relevant references stored in tables in real time.

Example: User asks in Joule: "Find me a customer reference in the financial services industry who successfully replaced Oracle." Joule hands off to ReferenceGPT. ReferenceGPT finds the match, surfaces the reference profile, and returns the reference with contact details - all within seconds.

Navigating Technical Challenges

  • Capability deployment: Building this wasn't a one-click setup. It required navigating custom capability deployment to Joule process (DEV → TEST → PREPROD → PROD), PR approvals, and getting principal propagation set up across each environment. If you are building your code-base agents from scratch by leveraging the AI Golden Factory Path framework, it’s easier to have an A2A connection. In our case, we already had our own existing backend
  • Scenario activation: Joule uses an LLM to decide whether to hand off to ReferenceGPT. Initially, all activation tests were failing - Joule kept choosing to answer directly instead of routing to our agent. The fix? The scenario description needs to closely mirror how users naturally phrase their questions.

We rewrote ours to: "Identify and retrieve qualified SAP reference customers for sales opportunities. Search customers by fit criteria (industry, company size, region, technology), retrieve proof points (customer quotes, success stories, case studies), and connect with field contacts for customer reference validation." 

Result: 16 of 20 activation tests now pass consistently. Refer to the help Guide to test if your scenario is activated in Joule

  • Multi-turn conversation ("Show more"): Initially our follow-up prompt to the agent (e.g. "Show more") was treated as a brand-new query and didn’t carry forward the previous prompt context to ReferenceGPT. Since we show 5 references at a time, users typing "show more" weren't getting relevant follow-up results. Fix? Joule allows the capability to store conversational memory on your end using contextId and taskId from Joule multi-turn API
  • Joule’s UI card integration: You can display Joule response in Joule UI cards or simply text format. We decided against it because it's too inflexible for our use case but worth exploring as it may be useful in other scenarios.
  • Model choice matters: We tested both Claude Sonnet and GPT-4o as the LLM powering ReferenceGPT inside Joule. Claude Sonnet produced better quality but added latency (still within Joule's 60-second limit). Worth testing for your own use case.

Ready to start? See Joule A2A help guides

  • Code-based Agent Integration (Bring your own agent)
  • Bring Your Own Agents to Joule via A2A
  • ReferenceGPT A2A GitHub repo (as example)

Tips

  • Raise Joule Standard team issues via the internal ITSM ticket (CA-JOULE-CR) and refer to the Joule troubleshooting guide.
  • For principal propagation issues or to establish trust and do attribute mapping, get support from the Platform CoE team by creating tickets here.
  • Joule usage tracking: Usage can be viewed at the Joule Usage Dashboard. Filtering specific document collection is not currently supported. However, for an A2A agent scenario, it can be tracked by filtering the scenario name.

Where we go from here

We're continuing to expand CMI's presence inside Joule with CMI agents and MCPs - adding more intelligence sources, improving response quality, and exploring new ways to bring the right insight to the right person at the right moment. A2A isn't just about integration - it's about letting each tool do what it was designed to do, then orchestrating them to create something greater than the sum of its parts

Special thanks to the Joule team ( Kristiana Dimova, Rumyana Vaseva, Dragomir Drashkov, Alex Polevoy), CMI leadership ( Franklin Herbas, Paul Logue) , CPIT ( Damien Garnier, Christoph Kommer) and Global References team (John Gruber, Paul Smith, Kevin Scanlon)

entry.title: Beyond the Standard: Developing the "CPI Kafka Adapter Plus" for High-Volume Event Streaming
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

I’m Florian Kube. When I look back at my career, I’ve been working in the SAP integration space for over 10 years now. I briefly started with SAP PI/PO, but jumped straight onto HCI (now SAP Integration Suite) as soon as it became available - all the way back when we still had to configure flows using the Eclipse plugin.

In recent years, the integration landscape has shifted. Synchronous request-response setups are increasingly replaced by modern, event-driven architectures. Event-driven integration and streaming are no longer just buzzwords; they are part of our daily work now. And when we talk about streaming in the enterprise space, the conversation almost always leads to Apache Kafka (next to SAP Advanced Event Mesh 😉).

In theory, both Apache Kafka and SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) are built to process messages immediately and in real-time. That is the core beauty of event-driven design. However, when we apply this high-frequency, single-message pattern directly to CPI in high-volume enterprise environments, we quickly run into practical operational and performance challenges.

To solve these practical challenges, I started an open-source project: the CPI Kafka Adapter Plus.

 High-Frequency Events Meet Operational Reality

When systems exchange data, enterprise architectures often use bulk processing. However, when handling high-frequency events or large data streams in CPI, treating every single event as an individual integration transaction creates major operational issues:

  1. MPL Overload & The Logging Dilemma: When consuming events individually, every single event processed by CPI creates its own Message Processing Log (MPL). To handle this in high-volume scenarios, you could set the log level to "Error Only". But let's be honest: in daily operations, that often feels like flying blind. It frequently leads to misunderstandings and troubleshooting difficulties because you lose all visibility on successful processing. On the other hand, keeping standard logs turned on with high message volumes can overload the database, degrade performance, and clutter your monitoring.

  2. Downstream System Overload: Even if CPI can ingest thousands of high-frequency events without issues, the receiving target system (like an ERP database or a legacy API) often cannot. Flooding a downstream system with thousands of individual, tiny requests can easily overwhelm it. Gathering these events into structured packages is often a technical necessity to protect downstream systems.

  3. Cost Efficiency (And how SAP counts messages): In SAP Integration Suite, licensing is billed per processed message, where each 250KB of payload counts as one message. If you push 1,000 tiny single-event messages (even if they are only 1KB each) through CPI, SAP counts and bills this as 1,000 messages. However, if you bundle those 1,000 events into a single 1MB bulk package, it is processed as a single transaction and, based on the 250KB rule, only counts as 4 messages! Processing unbatched, high-frequency single events is an area where you can save a lot of money with the right optimization.

The Previous Workaround

To optimize this, integration developers often have to build workarounds. For example, when a sending system like SAP's Data Replication Framework (DRF) sends business objects as a bulk payload, the standard Kafka adapter cannot automatically split this bulk into individual Kafka records.

As a workaround, developers have to build tedious splitter logic in the Integration Flow to split the bulk and send every single message individually to Kafka. While this works, it bloats the integration flows with unnecessary processing steps, forces CPI into long processing loops, and generates very large log files.

An Adapter to Bridge the Gap

Why not move this efficiency logic directly into the adapter? The goal was to build a custom adapter that respects Kafka’s native streaming design while protecting CPI’s operational and monitoring boundaries.

The CPI Kafka Adapter Plus was designed to:

  • Support native batching directly out of the box.

  • Keep integration flows clean by avoiding complex splitters and aggregators.

  • Drastically reduce the CPI resource footprint and MPL count without having to resort to restrictive "Error Only" logging.

Accelerating Adapter Development with AI

As an Integration Architect, my core focus is on system design, architecture, and orchestration, rather than spending days writing raw Java boilerplate.

Developing a custom adapter for SAP CPI requires working with the Adapter Development Kit (ADK). While the ADK is highly powerful, it is also a proprietary, specialized framework with a steep learning curve. Finding extensive community examples or templates for complex ADK implementations is incredibly difficult.

Instead of letting this framework barrier slow me down, I treated Generative AI (Claude and GitHub Copilot) as my virtual pair-programmers. With a clear architectural blueprint in mind, this modern developer workflow allowed me to build the CPI Kafka Adapter Plus with maximum efficiency:

  • Navigating the ADK Lifecycle: I used Claude to clarify the underlying lifecycle methods of the ADK. I could describe the exact architectural behavior I wanted (such as holding a session open to collect messages in a custom thread before pushing them to the pipeline), and Claude translated these concepts into the structure required by the framework.

  • Automating the Boilerplate: I used GitHub Copilot to speed up the repetitive coding. It helped generate Maven configurations, set up properties, and draft unit tests. This kept me focused entirely on the core business and routing logic of the adapter.

  • Resolving Complex Dependencies: Merging Kafka client libraries, Avro schemas, and JSON validation libraries into the strict runtime environment of an SAP adapter can easily lead to classpath conflicts. AI helped me quickly troubleshoot these tricky packaging errors, saving hours of searching through legacy forums.

This experience showed me that with a strong architectural vision, modern AI tools can bridge the gap between design and implementation. It allowed me to deliver an enterprise-grade adapter without having to reinvent the wheel for every line of boilerplate code.

What Can the "Kafka Adapter Plus" Do?

The CPI Kafka Adapter Plus is not just a "Proof of Concept." It has already been running stable in production for months. Primarily in use cases involving massive amounts of data.

Of course, all of these features are completely modular and optional. You can use just a single capability or combine them however you like - whether you only need Avro serialization, only require bulk processing, or want to mix and match them depending on your specific integration requirements.

Here are the key features that helped bridge the gap between high-speed streaming and SAP CPI:

1. Batching & Bulk Processing

Whether you are sending bulk data to Kafka or consuming high-frequency events from it, the CPI Kafka Adapter Plus supports native batching in both directions:

  • On the Receiver Side (sending to Kafka): When business objects (like those from SAP DRF) arrive in CPI as a bulk, you no longer need to split them inside your flow. The adapter allows you to transform and hand over the bulk payload in a single step. The CPI Kafka Adapter Plus then automatically streams them to Kafka as native, individual records. This completely avoids tedious splitter or looping steps within the Integration Flow, preventing resource-heavy loops and drastically reducing transaction logging overhead.

  • On the Sender Side (consuming from Kafka): Instead of triggering thousands of individual Integration Flow executions (and thus thousands of individual MPLs) for every single Kafka event, the adapter can bundle incoming messages into a single bulk package. This dramatically reduces the MPL count, preserves tenant resources, and lowers the counted message volume, while still keeping your monitoring clean and manageable.

 

2. Dead Letter Topic  Support

When it comes to error handling, standard Kafka adapters often rely on simple retry loops. If a message is corrupt or cannot be processed, it triggers rapid retry cycles. Since the default retry interval is usually very short (often in the range of seconds), CPI gets stuck repeatedly processing the exact same failing message over and over again. Wasting valuable CPU resources and cluttering up your logs.

The CPI Kafka Adapter Plus offers Dead Letter Topic support. If processing a message fails, it can be cleanly routed to a defined Dead Letter Topic instead of looping infinitely, allowing the rest of the message stream to continue running uninterrupted.

 

3. Integrated Avro Serialization

Anyone using Kafka professionally usually relies on Apache Avro for schema definition. Typically, achieving this in CPI requires making manual HTTP calls to an external Schema Registry and writing complex Groovy scripts to handle the serialization and deserialization. This approach is highly error-prone and unnecessarily bloats your Integration Flows.

The CPI Kafka Adapter Plus solves this by offering native Avro serialization right out of the box, completely eliminating the need for custom scripts and HTTP overhead in your message flow.

 

4. JSON Validation

The CPI Kafka Adapter Plus validates JSON payloads directly against predefined schemas. Both when sending and receiving messages. This means invalid data is caught and filtered out right at the adapter boundary, preventing corrupt payloads from ever entering and disrupting your CPI integration flows.

Open Source: Try It Out and Join In!

I firmly believe in the power of the SAP community. That’s why I decided to make the entire source code of the CPI Kafka Adapter Plus available as open source.

If you are facing similar challenges with mass data and Kafka in the SAP Integration Suite, I warmly invite you to try out the CPI Kafka Adapter Plus:

  • GitHub Repository: FinkeFlo/cpi-kafka-adapter-plus

  • Documentation & Setup Guide: FinkeFlo GitHub Pages

I would love to get your feedback, feature requests, or even pull requests directly on GitHub. Let’s make the SAP integration world a little better and more efficient together!

How do you currently handle mass data challenges with Kafka in CPI? Let me know in the comments below!

Best regards,

Florian

Disclaimer: This is an independent, community-driven open-source project. It is not an official SAP product, and is not officially supported or endorsed by SAP SE. Use at your own discretion.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM: How to Print Only One GR Label Regardless of Receipt Quantity
entry.content:
Hi All,

We have a requirement in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud regarding GR label printing and would appreciate your guidance.

When posting a Goods Receipt for an Inbound Delivery, the business requires the system to automatically print one GR label per inbound delivery line item, regardless of any quantity maintained on the document, such as:

Order Quantity
Entry Quantity
Storage Quantity
Order Price Quantity

The key requirement is that the label determination must be automatic and always result in exactly one label per line item.

I am aware of the "Number of Labels" field during PO Goods Receipt processing; however, this does not meet the requirement because it relies on manual user input rather than system-driven determination.

So far, I have explored the following options without success:

Output Parameter Determination - Adjusted the number of print copies. This only controls how many copies of an already-generated label are printed and does not influence the number of labels generated.

Label Form Assignment in the Product Master - Tested alternative label forms. All available label configurations appear to remain quantity-driven and do not support a fixed one label per item approach.

Number of GR Slips Configuration - Tested different values. The result is still calculated based on the receipt quantity and is not a 1:1 determination per line item.

Has anyone encountered a similar requirement in Public Cloud?

Any guidance or experience would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

James.

entry.title: Document Grounding | Add context to Joule Q&A using information retrieved from custom documents
entry.content:
Document Grounding empowers SAP customers to get accurate, trustworthy answers from Joule by securely grounding conversations in their own business documents. Instead of relying solely on generic language model knowledge, Joule uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to pull relevant information from unstructured and semi-structured documents stored in SAP and approved third-party repositories.

This ensures responses are context-aware, factually correct, and based on the most up-to-date business data.

By tracing answers back to their source documents, Document Grounding increases transparency and accountability while enabling more reliable, use-case-specific insights directly within conversational workflows.

 
Benefits
  • Improve user self-reliance and user satisfaction
  • Improve the reliability and applicability of generated content
  • Reduce hallucinations or incorrect generated information

Set Up Document Grounding

The complete setup guide is available here Setup document grounding

 

Related Information
Metering and Pricing
Supported Regions

entry.title: Re: PoC > 100% in Fixed Price Projects – Event-Based Reve...
entry.content:
Hello @Tilak3 ,

the observed behavior is the S/4HANA Cloud ERP standard one. However, during month end closing you are running the event based revenue recognition job, this will in this case reduce the recognized revenue to the sold revenue (amount to be billed in the billing plan of the billing item).

If you change the project status to "Completed" the event based revenue recognition will change the recognized revenue to the billed revenue.

In the context of fix price projects it is best practice to revise the project plan or maintain a forecast via "Review Customer Projects" on a regular (most cases monthly) basis. This ensures that the percentage of completion is valid and therefore the correct revenue is recognized.

Note: within fix price projects all hours/cost are treated the same, independent if the role and time entry is billable or non-billable.

Best regards,

David
